feedback.text: In our current era, online consumption is the main form of consumption of all kinds (entertainment, news, social interaction, and gradually until daily consumer goods such as toilet or food products are traded), and this has become a source of all types of scams, deception, theft, misleading transactions and other forms of harm to consumers and producers. Growing problems such as the monopoly of payment processors trying to decide by the consumer on which products and where they can buy, the lack of clarity about the possession of products and media on online platforms by allowing businesses to sell something of what the consumer is never ‘in possession’ and which is a product or service as it is more favourable to them (a problem that the Stop Killing Games initiative has managed to bring to public awareness), fraudulent sales in online shops and the lack of means to recover the losses they entail, the user’s exploitation as a source of data is either a source of data for the resale of the latter or for the training of artificial toys that replace us as a worker/creative, and they have been able to cope with unfair market places, as well as to find themselves in a state of play as a trader. If Europeans really want to enter the 21st century and look at the 22nd century, we must ensure that online trade is truly free and safe, without the producer or consumer having to submit to what simple intermediaries who try to bring the market only for their own benefit decide, but we are pushing for the online market to become actively harmful and for grey and black markets to appear that offer the services that “legal” markets do not offer (physical or digital possession of digital media, access to legal content but which platforms refuse to offer, privacy and user’s right to anonymity, preservation of digital media and works, etc.). Gabriel Newell, creator of Steam (the most successful online platform for video games and digital media) made a number of years on how to stop digital piracy: the user must be offered a quality service, which the pirate cannot match. Similarly, if digital services are of such poor quality and so unsafe that piracy is a better way to access these media and content, the market becomes impossible to maintain. I hope that in the coming years I will not be forced to travel to countries outside the EU if I want to read a novel whose topics are considered “politically incorrect” or see a film that has ceased to be in distribution, but if this is or nothing for consumers, we will not have any other remedy.
